rationale: The citing paper uses Diehl (2005) as key prior evidence that consumers can over-search and that additional search can worsen outcomes, specifically in ordered, agent-ranked environments. It then explicitly positions its own contribution as adding to that research stream by identifying feature nonalignability as another cause of over-search and declining outcomes. This is more than a passing background mention because it helps justify the slippery-slope hypotheses and the paper's claimed contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Diehl (2005)'s over-search finding from ordered recommendation contexts to a different mechanism: alignability-driven search among options. It broadens the phenomenon by showing that nonalignable features can induce continued search past the satisfaction-maximizing point.
